## Question

What causes earthquakes?

response_text: ANSWER: Earthquakes are primarily caused by the sudden release of accumulated stress in Earth’s crust, most often along faults where tectonic plates interact; as plates grind past, collide, or pull apart, rocks deform until they rupture (elastic rebound), generating seismic waves from a subsurface focus (hypocenter) that radiate to the surface (epicenter). Most occur at plate boundaries: transform faults produce strike-slip quakes, convergent margins and subduction zones generate powerful thrust and megathrust earthquakes (sometimes causing tsunamis), and divergent boundaries yield normal-fault quakes at rifts and mid-ocean ridges; quakes also happen within plates on reactivated ancient faults. Additional causes include volcanic magma movement, landslides or collapses, isostatic rebound after ice melt, large reservoir impoundment, deep mining, wastewater injection or hydraulic fracturing (induced seismicity), and rare meteor impacts, with aftershocks reflecting ongoing fault readjustment.
